summaries of two equities: "26/7: Coca-Cola (KO US, Buy, Target Price USD74, Upside: +17%) (*Awaiting research analyst earnings update)
2Q organic sales (+11% yoy) and EPS (+8% yoy) beat, vs. consensus +8.4% and +7.4% respectively 
Beat was driven by 10% growth price/mix and 1% and 1% growth in concentrate sales
Raised FY organic sales guidance to +8-9% (from 7-8%) vs. consensus of +8.4% 
Higher net price realization in Ems where it continues to maintain positive volumes 
" and "14/7: PepsiCo (PEP US, Buy, Target Price USD225, Upside: +19%)
2Q organic revenue (+13% yoy) and EPS (+12% yoy) beat, vs consensus +9.8% and +5.3% respectively 
Beat was also driven by robust pricing growth, at +15% y/y in 2Q, vs +16% in 1Q
CFRA updated outlook (TP from USD220 to USD225) on 2Q beat, citing faster growing international markets and the Frito-Lay business as the primary growth drivers
10% increase in its annualized dividend starting June, the 51st straight year to hike dividend"

Coca-Cola:
Coca-Cola reported strong second-quarter results, with organic sales and earnings per share beating expectations. The beat was driven by growth in price/mix and concentrate sales. The company also raised its full-year organic sales guidance. Coca-Cola continues to maintain positive volumes in the Ems region, partly due to higher net price realization.

PepsiCo:
PepsiCo also reported impressive second-quarter results, with organic revenue and earnings per share surpassing consensus estimates. The beat was fueled by robust pricing growth and strong performance in international markets and the Frito-Lay business. Additionally, the company announced a 10% increase in its annualized dividend, marking the 51st consecutive year of dividend hikes.

Coca-Cola:
- 2Q organic sales and EPS beat expectations, with +11% and +8% YoY growth respectively
- Beat driven by 10% growth in price/mix and 1% growth in concentrate sales
- Raised FY organic sales guidance to +8-9%
- Maintaining positive volumes in Ems region with higher net price realization

PepsiCo:
- 2Q organic revenue and EPS exceeded expectations, with +13% and +12% YoY growth respectively
- Beat driven by robust pricing growth (+15% YoY)
- CFRA updated outlook and raised target price on strong 2Q performance
- 10% increase in annualized dividend, marking 51 straight years of dividend hikes
make it short and concise for every point

Coca-Cola:
- 2Q sales and EPS beat expectations: +11% sales, +8% EPS YoY
- Beat driven by 10% price/mix growth and 1% concentrate sales
- Raised FY organic sales guidance to +8-9%

PepsiCo:
- 2Q revenue and EPS exceeded expectations: +13% revenue, +12% EPS YoY
- Beat driven by robust pricing growth: +15% YoY
- CFRA upgraded outlook and raised target price
- 10% increase in annualized dividend, 51st consecutive year of hikes.

summarise the news into three parts (brief headline, detail of news and government or staff words e.g. CEO/CFO, forecast, if any change number of policy e.g. funding/license/rate). The content of news: "US Consumer Sentiment Rises to Highest Since 2021 as Prices Ease
Friday, July 28, 2023 10:36 PM 
By Augusta Saraiva
* University of Michigan index climbed to 71.6 this month
* Fewer consumers blame inflation for eroding living standards
(Bloomberg) --US consumer sentiment rose in July to the highest since October 2021 as inflation continued to ease.
The sentiment index climbed to 71.6 from 64.4 last month, according to the final July reading from the University of Michigan. The median estimate in a Bloomberg survey of economists called for 72.6, in line with the group’s print earlier in the month.


Consumers expect prices will climb at an annual rate of 3.4% over the next year, up slightly from the 3.3% expected in June, data Friday showed. They still see costs rising 3% over the next five to 10 years.
Americans are growing more optimistic about the economy as wage gains are finally outpacing inflation and unemployment remains low. Separate data out Friday showed key metrics of US inflation are easing, adding to growing optimism the economy can avoid a recession.
The share of consumers blaming high prices for eroding their living standards fell to 36%, the lowest reading in a year and a half, according to the report. However, this measure for lower-income consumers rose in the month.
“This re-emerging divergence between high- and low-income consumers, if sustained, restores the typical pattern in which higher-income consumers have more favorable sentiment,” Joanne Hsu, director of the survey, said in a statement. “The months ahead will reveal if the recovery in sentiment will be shared across the income distribution.”
The final July reading was somewhat weaker than the one earlier in the month. Since then, gas prices have risen significantly due to a combination of unexpected refinery outages and lower-than-normal stockpiles in key storage hubs, which may risk reigniting inflation.
The current conditions gauge climbed to 76.6 from 69 in June, while a measure of expectations also advanced, the university said. Buying conditions for durable goods improved to the highest level in two years."

Headline: US Consumer Sentiment at Highest since 2021 as Inflation Eases
- University of Michigan index rose to 71.6 in July
- Consumers less likely to blame inflation for lower living standards
- Inflation expectations at 3.4% for next year, 3% for next five to ten years
- Optimism about the economy increases as wage gains outpace inflation and unemployment remains low
- Lower-income consumers show higher blame for high prices affecting living standards
- Final July reading slightly weaker than earlier in the month, potentially due to rising gas prices
- Current conditions gauge and expectations also improved
- Buying conditions for durable goods at highest level in two years
combine  parts into one single paragraph and make the content short and concise. Limit the words to less than 70 words. Replace the word of "analyst" to "consensus"

US consumer sentiment rose to its highest level since October 2021 as inflation continued to ease. The University of Michigan index climbed to 71.6 in July, slightly lower than the consensus estimate of 72.6. Consumers expect prices to rise at a 3.4% annual rate over the next year. The share of consumers blaming high prices for lower living standards decreased to its lowest reading in a year and a half. However, lower-income consumers showed higher concern.

BEIJING — China’s housing ministry has announced plans to make it easier for people to buy property.

The news, out late Thursday, indicates how different levels of government are starting to act just days after Beijing signaled a shift away from its crackdown on real estate speculation.

The planned measures include easing purchase restrictions for people wanting to buy a second house, and reducing down payment ratios for first-time homebuyers, according to an article on the Ministry of Housing and Urban-Rural Development’s website.

In an effort to reduce speculation in its massive property market, China has made it much harder for people to buy a second house.

Mortgage rates for the second purchase can be a full percentage point higher than for the first, while the second-home down payment ratio can skyrocket to 70% or 80% in large cities, according to Natixis.

China's recent Politburo meeting had a more constructive outcome
China’s recent Politburo outcome was more constructive than market expected: GS
The housing ministry article referred to comments from its minister Ni Hong at a recent meeting with eight state-owned and non-state-owned companies in construction and real estate.

Since it was a meeting at the central government ministry level, it did not discuss policies for individual cities, said Bruce Pang, chief economist and head of research for Greater China at JLL.

But he expects Beijing will encourage local governments to announce real estate policy changes that fit their specific situation. Pang also pointed out that including construction companies at the meeting emphasized their role in promoting investment and stabilizing growth.

Waiting on details
China has not yet announced formal measures for supporting real estate. However, top level leaders on Monday signaled a greater focus on housing demand, rather than supply.

On Tuesday, China’s State Taxation Administration announced “guidelines” for waiving or reducing housing-related taxes. It was not immediately clear what implementation would look like for home buyers.

We continue to expect the property sector rally to continue and advise investors to focus on beta names within the property sector.
Jizhou Dong
NOMURA
The readout of Monday’s Politburo meeting also removed the phrase “houses are for living in, not speculation,” which has been a mantra for Beijing’s tight stance and efforts to rein in developers’ high reliance on debt for growth.

“It seems to us that [the housing ministry] is quick in response this time and also gets bolder on relaxing property policies,” Jizhou Dong, China property research analyst at Nomura, said in a note Friday.

Given such speed, Dong expects markets are anticipating specific policy implementation in cities such as Shanghai or Guangzhou.

Read more about China from CNBC Pro
Goldman names China stocks set for a bounce — and 2 make its list of top buy-rated picks

Morgan Stanley reveals 6 of its top China stocks, including a chipmaker it expects to soar 80%

What China’s resilient big spenders are buying and the stocks that benefit

The chip wars are heating up. This Chinese stock rose 30% in five days

Hong Kong-traded Chinese property stocks such as Longfor
, Country Garden
 and Greentown China
 traded higher Friday, on pace to close out the week with gains after plunging on Monday over debt worries.

“We continue to expect the property sector rally to continue and advise investors to focus on beta names within the property sector,” Nomura’s Dong said.

Those stocks include U.S.-listed Ke Holdings
, as well as Hong Kong-listed Longfor and China Overseas Land and Investment
, the report said, noting Nomura has a “buy” rating on all three.

“We still advise investors to stay away from weaker privately-owned developers.”

China's housing ministry is planning to ease restrictions on property purchases, including reducing down payment ratios for first-time homebuyers and easing purchase restrictions for second homes. This comes after Beijing signaled a shift away from its crackdown on real estate speculation. The move aims to promote housing demand and stabilize growth. While specific policy implementation is awaited, Chinese property stocks such as Longfor, Country Garden, and Greentown China are expected to benefit, according to Nomura. However, caution is advised with weaker privately-owned developers.

write me the performance of market perfomance (HSI and CSI 300) and the performance of KWEB and HXC: "Whether investors actually believe what Beijing is saying this time around, what's true is that the "supports" are being ramped up continuously, and asset prices are reacting with volumes. HSI gained 7.4% from the lows last Monday, and CSI300 +5.3% with $5.5bn of NB inflow in the same 4-day period. Friday's reversal was especially meaningful given it came off a weak overnight tape/open print, and volumes @ HK$138bn the 2nd highest in 6 wks, just behind of Tuesday's explosive move. The other big plus has been the light/short positioning, while almost every other major markets are trading near highs.

 

HSI is set to test 20K today as KWEB +7.6%, HXC +7.0% saw investors FOMOing into the China trade, while SPX also closed at a fresh YTD high. Key event today is NDRC's briefing on measures to boost consumption at 3pm {NSN RYLOLKT1UM0W <GO>} - I suspect that should keep the bears at bay today, and probably drive even more short covering in the consumer plays. However, given the very sharp move up we've got already, I'd expect to see a breather sometime later this week to consolidate above 20K (bullish). Excuse for that could be weak data {NSN RYHOO7T1UM0W <GO>} even though we kind of know that already..."

The market performance has been positive with the Hang Seng Index (HSI) gaining 7.4% from its lows last Monday and the CSI300 up 5.3%. The HSI is expected to test 20,000 today, supported by strong gains in KWEB (+7.6%) and HXC (+7.0%) as investors show FOMO (fear of missing out) in the China trade. The market has been reacting to continuous support measures from Beijing, with high volumes and light/short positioning. The SPX also closed at a fresh year-to-date high. The key event today is the National Development and Reform Commission's briefing on measures to boost consumption, expected to drive short-covering and keep the bears at bay. A consolidation above 20,000 is anticipated, although a breather may occur later in the week.
make the content short and concise. Limit the words to less than 70 words. Replace the word of "analyst" to "consensus"

HSI and CSI300 gained 7.4% and 5.3% respectively. KWEB (+7.6%) and HXC (+7.0%) saw increased investor interest in China. Market responds positively to Beijing's continuous supports. High volumes and light/short positioning contribute. HSI expected to test 20K. NDRC briefing on consumption measures likely to drive short-covering. Market may consolidate above 20K. Weak data could lead to a breather later this week. SPX closes at YTD high.

make the tone to be professional written and same tone: "China update: policy supports news  (also market performance , tell the sentiment of KWEB, HXC ) + PMI data 
The National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) has issued a notice regarding measures to restore and expand consumption, including in the automotive and real estate sectors. The notice states that localities are prohibited from implementing new car purchase restrictions and should optimize existing restrictions based on local conditions. It emphasizes the promotion of the cancellation of second-hand car migration restrictions and facilitation of second-hand car transaction registration. Discriminatory policies towards non-local production cars are not allowed. The notice also highlights support for digital consumption, improvement of consumer infrastructure, and increased financial support for various sectors. The development of the REITs market and optimization of consumer credit rates and terms are mentioned as well.
China’s Housing Ministry plans to ease property purchase restrictions, allowing easier access to second homes and reducing down payment ratios for first-time homebuyers. The move comes after Beijing’s signal of a shift away from its crackdown on real estate speculation. Details and implementation of the measures are yet to be announced, but local governments are expected to make city-specific policy changes. 
Market responds positively to Beijing’s continuous supports. HSI gained 7.4% from the lows last Monday, and CSI300 +5.3% with $5.5bn of NB inflow in the same 4-day period. KWEB (+7.6%) and HXC (+7.0%) saw increased investor interest in China.
The National Bureau of Statistics and China Federation of Logistics and Purchasing have released monthly figures. In July, the manufacturing Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI) increased to 49.3 from 49 in June, surpassing expectations of 48.9. However, the non-manufacturing PMI decreased to 51.5 from 53.2 in June, falling short of the estimated 53. 

US: core PCE / spending macro data + Biden plans 
US economy shows signs of soft landing with PCE, employment costs, and consumer spending data. Dow Jones rises for 13th consecutive day, matching the longest streak since 1987. In June, PCE price index and employment cost index had lowest increases since 2021, suggesting cooling inflation. Adjusted for inflation, consumer spending in June had highest growth rate since January. University of Michigan's consumer confidence index reached near two-year high in July.
Biden plans restrictions on US tech investments in China, focusing on semiconductors, AI, and quantum computing. Existing investments unaffected, but some transactions banned, others require disclosure. Measures effective next year, part of broader effort to curb China’s tech development. Yellen assures targeted control, minimal impact on China’s investment prospects. China warns of potential retaliation. Senate passes amendment for firms to report investments in China and other concerning countries, as lawmakers seek stronger action.
Japan: YCC key headlines
Although the BoJ kept the reference points of +/- 50bp around 0.00% for 10-year JGB yields, fixed-rate purchases have now been set at 1.0%, meaning that the BoJ has implemented a de-facto widening of the YCC band. At the accompanying press conference, Governor Ueda mentioned that today’s move was a pre-emptive move to handle future risks such as those stemming from market speculation, while maintaining his stance that monetary easing is needed. 
"

China: 
- NDRC issues notice on measures to restore and expand consumption
- Housing Ministry plans to ease property purchase restrictions
- Market responds positively, HSI and CSI300 gain, investor interest in KWEB and HXC increases
- July manufacturing PMI surpasses expectations, non-manufacturing PMI falls below estimate

US: 
- PCE, employment cost, and consumer spending data suggest soft landing for the economy
- Dow Jones rises for 13th consecutive day
- Biden plans restrictions on US tech investments in China
- Consumer confidence reaches near two-year high in July

Japan:
- BoJ implements de-facto widening of YCC band
- Governor Ueda mentions move as a pre-emptive measure to handle future risks
